title: Friends & Family

vocab.bond: a close connection or relationship between people
vocab.rely on: to depend on someone for help or support
vocab.get on with: to have a friendly relationship with someone
vocab.extended family: all the people in a family group, including cousins, aunts, and uncles

phrases: On the one hand... on the other hand...

questions: Do you have a large family?
How often do you see your relatives?
What do you usually do with your friends at the weekend?
Who do you get on with best in your family?
Have your friendships changed much over the years?
answers: I would say my family is quite average. I have one sibling, my parents, and we see my grandparents regularly, especially during holidays.
I see my close relatives about once a month. My cousins live in another city, so we usually meet for birthdays or special occasions.
At the weekend, I normally meet my friends at a cafe or go to the cinema. Sometimes we just hang out at someone's house and chat.
I probably get on best with my older sister because we share a lot of interests and she gives me good advice about school and friends.
Yes, some friendships have changed. I am still close to my childhood friends, but I have also made new friends through hobbies and school activities.

part2.question: What is important for a strong friendship?
part2.photoA: Two friends laughing together while having coffee
part2.photoB: Two friends supporting each other during a difficult moment
part2.model: In photo A, two friends are enjoying themselves and laughing over coffee. This suggests that fun and shared interests are important in a friendship. In photo B, one friend is clearly supporting the other, which shows that trust and emotional support matter too. I think both elements are essential. A strong friendship needs laughter, but it also needs someone you can rely on when life is hard. Personally, I value trust more because a fun friend is not enough if they disappear when you need them.

part3.task: A youth club wants to help teenagers build better friendships. Discuss the ideas and decide which two would be most useful.
part3.model:
A: I think group sports would be great because people bond when they work towards a common goal.
B: That's a good point, but not everyone likes sport. Workshops on communication skills could help shy students make friends more easily.
A: I agree. And team-building trips might create strong memories, although they are expensive.
B: Volunteering projects are also powerful because people connect when they help others together.
A: So maybe communication workshops and volunteering would reach the most people.
B: Yes, I agree with you.

questions: Is it better to have a few close friends or many acquaintances?
Should parents choose their children's friends?
How has social media changed friendships?
What role should family play in a teenager's life?
answers: I would say a few close friends are better because quality matters more than quantity. Close friends are the ones who really know you and support you.
I don't think parents should choose their children's friends. Teenagers need to learn how to judge people themselves, although parents can give advice.
Social media has made it easier to stay in touch, but I think it can also make friendships more shallow. Real connection needs face-to-face time.
Family should provide a safe base and emotional support, but they also need to give teenagers freedom to become independent.

homework.reading: Read a short story about friendship. Identify one conflict and how it was resolved.
homework.listening: Listen to a podcast episode about family relationships. Write down three new expressions you hear.
homework.writing: Write an essay (140–190 words): 'Is it more important to spend time with family or friends?'
